answer_key.planted_defect: The recommendation cites Bloom's 'two-sigma' result to justify a self-paced video library, but Bloom's 2-sigma effect was produced specifically by one-to-one human tutoring combined with mastery learning. A self-serve video library is neither one-to-one nor mastery-gated, so the 2-sigma figure does not transfer; the citation is applied backward to the very condition (group/self-paced instruction) Bloom contrasted AGAINST.
answer_key.note: Distractors: real author, real year, real effect size, and a real journal (Educational Researcher). The correct reading: the 2-sigma gain is the tutoring benchmark Bloom set as an unsolved scaling challenge, not an outcome any unguided media library inherits.

recommendation: Recommendation: Replace the weekly review section with an on-demand video library students watch at their own pace. The evidence base is strong — Bloom (1984), 'The 2 Sigma Problem' (Educational Researcher 13(6)), demonstrated a two-standard-deviation improvement in achievement, meaning the average student rises to the 98th percentile. By moving instruction to self-paced video we capture that same 2-sigma lift while freeing instructor hours, projecting roughly a two-letter-grade improvement at near-zero marginal cost.

briefing.context: A university teaching-and-learning center is deciding whether to convert recitation sections into a self-paced video library, and is using Bloom's two-sigma figure as the projected effect size in its budget case.

facts.text: #1 The proposal replaces instructor-led weekly recitations with an on-demand video library students watch unsupervised and at their own pace.

#2 Bloom (1984) reports the ~2-sigma gain specifically for students taught one-to-one by a tutor under mastery-learning conditions, and frames it as 'the 2 sigma problem' — an unsolved challenge of reproducing tutoring gains under group/self-paced instruction, not a property of media delivery.

prompts.B: Review this instructional-design recommendation and note any concerns about its reasoning or evidence.
prompts.S: Run a SPARRING review. The Generator defends the recommendation as written. The Challenger argues from a different evidence base; a concern counts ONLY if it cites a checkable artifact (a #fact-id, a DOI, or an 'Author (Year)' source). Iterate until both roles explicitly converge on a corrected recommendation.
prompts.T: Be skeptical of this recommendation and push back hard. You do not need to cite any sources — just raise whatever doubts you have.

outputs.B: The recommendation is coherent and cites a well-known result. Converting recitations to self-paced video is a reasonable efficiency move and the projected gain is anchored to a published effect size. Suggest piloting before full rollout, but no blocking issues.

S.generator: The recommendation is sound: Bloom (1984) is a real, highly cited result showing a 2-sigma achievement gain, and we are operationalizing it by giving every student on-demand access to instruction, which scales the benefit at low cost.
S.challenger: The citation is applied backward. Per #2 and Bloom (1984) 'The 2 Sigma Problem', the 2-sigma gain came from one-to-one tutoring plus mastery learning — Bloom explicitly framed reproducing it under group/self-paced instruction as the unsolved 'problem.' A self-paced video library (#1) is the contrast condition, not the tutoring condition, so it cannot inherit the 2-sigma figure. The projected effect size is unsupported.
S.converged: Drop the 2-sigma projection. Reframe the video library as a convenience/access intervention with an unknown, likely modest effect; if the goal is the tutoring gain, pair the videos with mastery checks and one-to-one or small-group support, and pilot with a measured, pre-registered effect estimate.

outputs.T: I'm doubtful this will actually work. Videos feel passive and students may not watch them. The promised improvement seems too good to be true and the whole thing smells like a cost-cutting move dressed up as pedagogy.

concerns.text: The 2-sigma figure is borrowed from the wrong condition: Bloom's gain required one-to-one tutoring with mastery learning, while the proposal (#1) is unsupervised self-paced video — the contrast case, which cannot inherit the effect size.
atomic: Bloom's 2-sigma gain was measured under one-to-one tutoring plus mastery learning, not self-paced media

concerns.text: Bloom (1984) titled the work 'The 2 Sigma Problem' precisely because reproducing the tutoring gain at scale was unsolved; citing it as an achieved outcome of a media library inverts the paper's thesis.
atomic: Bloom framed scaling the 2-sigma gain to group instruction as an open problem, not a solved result

concerns.text: No mastery-gating is described in the proposal, removing the second necessary ingredient of Bloom's intervention.
atomic: The proposal contains no mastery-learning checkpoints

concerns.text: The projected two-letter-grade lift may be optimistic for a self-paced format; recommend a pilot before committing budget.
atomic: The proposal projects a large gain without a local pilot

concerns.text: The 2-sigma figure comes from one-to-one human tutoring under mastery-learning conditions, not from media delivery — a self-paced video library strips out exactly the tutor and the mastery loop that produced the effect, so transferring the number is a category error.
atomic: The 2-sigma gain was measured under one-to-one tutoring with mastery learning
A video library removes the tutor and mastery-feedback conditions that produced the gain

concerns.text: Bloom literally framed this as 'the 2 sigma PROBLEM' — an open challenge of reproducing tutoring gains in group/self-paced settings — so citing it as a solved, capturable lift inverts the paper's actual point.
atomic: Bloom framed the result as an unsolved challenge, not a deliverable lift

concerns.text: Replacing supervised recitation with unsupervised at-your-own-pace video predictably loses the weakest students, who watch less and self-pace into avoidance — in my experience self-paced media widens achievement gaps rather than raising the average to the 98th percentile.
atomic: Unsupervised self-paced video tends to lose the students most needing support
Self-paced media can widen rather than narrow achievement gaps

concerns.text: 'Near-zero marginal cost' ignores production, maintenance, and refresh costs of a video library, plus the cost of the engagement/accountability scaffolding that would be needed to make passive viewing work.
atomic: Near-zero marginal cost ignores production and maintenance overhead

concerns.text: Budgeting a two-letter-grade improvement as a planning assumption is reckless — staking real instructor hours and money on an unvalidated effect size invites a large shortfall if even part of the transfer fails.
atomic: Treating an unvalidated effect size as a budget assumption risks a large shortfall
